coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive fluorescent carbon dot sensor for quantification of soluble and insoluble Cr(VI) in particulate matter
coredata.dc:creator: Adotey, Enoch Kwasi
Amouei Torkmahalleh, Mehdi
Tastanova, Lyazzat
Bekeshev, Amirbek
Shah, Dhawal
Hopke, Philip K.
Lee, Woojin
Balanay, Mannix P.
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates advanced functional materials to address the need for practical and affordable analytical techniques for monitoring large amounts of insoluble Cr(VI). N,S-doped fluorescent carbon dots (f-CDs) were fabricated through microwave synthesis, with an average diameter of 10 nm. These f-CDs were explored as potential sensors for detecting Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter (PM). Laboratory experiments yielded positive results, showing average recoveries of 106.0%, 102.3%, 96.4%, and 101.7% for PbCrO4, BaCrO4, CaCrO4, and (NH4)2CrO4, respectively. Applying the fluorescence method to field PM samples, a method detection limit (MDL) of 0.32 ng/m3 for total Cr(VI) quantification was achieved. The fluorescence decay of carbon dots remained stable over time, suggesting that Cr(VI) quenching primarily occurred due to the internal filter effect through a static quenching mechanism. These f-CDs exhibited advantageous properties, including affordability, solubility, luminescence, and sensitivity, positioning them as a promising alternative for Cr(VI) detection in ambient air particulates. This study contributes to further developing carbon-based functional materials for detecting metallic air pollutants.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive fluorescent carbon dot sensor for quantification of soluble and insoluble Cr(VI) in particulate matter

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates advanced functional materials to address the need for practical and affordable analytical techniques for monitoring large amounts of insoluble Cr(VI). N,S-doped fluorescent carbon dots (f-CDs) were fabricated through microwave synthesis, with an average diameter of 10 nm. These f-CDs were explored as potential sensors for detecting Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter (PM). Laboratory experiments yielded positive results, showing average recoveries of 106.0%, 102.3%, 96.4%, and 101.7% for PbCrO4, BaCrO4, CaCrO4, and (NH4)2CrO4, respectively. Applying the fluorescence method to field PM samples, a method detection limit (MDL) of 0.32 ng/m3 for total Cr(VI) quantification was achieved. The fluorescence decay of carbon dots remained stable over time, suggesting that Cr(VI) quenching primarily occurred due to the internal filter effect through a static quenching mechanism. These f-CDs exhibited advantageous properties, including affordability, solubility, luminescence, and sensitivity, positioning them as a promising alternative for Cr(VI) detection in ambient air particulates. This study contributes to further developing carbon-based functional materials for detecting metallic air pollutants.

The application of functional carbon dots to quantify soluble and insoluble Cr(VI) in ambient PM.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Although the literature has addressed the measurement of total Cr/Cr(VI) concentration in environmental samples [1], there is a significant gap in research on the potential application of functional carbon materials for the detection of Cr(VI) in ambient airborne particulate matter. Anthropogenic sources contribute to about 60–70% of atmospheric chromium emissions, while the remaining 30–40% comes from natural sources such as soil and rocks [2]. The predominant oxidation states of chromium in the environment are Cr(VI) and Cr(III) [2,3]. While Cr(III) is an essential micronutrient [4], its hexavalent form is a known human carcinogen [5]. Exposure to hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in industry and the environment has been associated with health problems such as nasal septum perforation, lung cancer, asthma, bronchitis, and pneumonia [6–8]. Anthropogenic sources of Cr(VI) include processes in ferrochromium plants, metallurgical industries, refractory manufacturing, electroplating, and fuel combustion [9,10]. Studies have found significantly higher concentrations of insoluble Cr(VI) than soluble Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter [11,12]. Insoluble Cr(VI) exhibits higher toxicity because it generates reactive radicals with high oxidation potential in the human body, potentially leading to mutations [4]. While soluble Cr(VI) can be reduced to Cr(III) by antioxidants in the lung fluid [13] and bloodstream [4], inhalable insoluble Cr(VI), once deposited in the lungs, can have chronic effects on human health. Hence, it is crucial to accurately measure the quantity of insoluble Cr(VI) in particulate matter for a precise evaluation of its actual impact on health.

In an effort to protect public health and the environment, various methods have been used to quantify different types of Cr in particulate matter, including ASTM Method D 5281–92, NIOSH 7605, and OSHA ID − 215 [14]. ASTM Method D 5281–92 involves the use of Greenberg-Smith impingers filled with NaHCO3 buffer to sample airborne particles, but its cumbersome nature limits its application for large-scale air monitoring campaigns [15–17]. Similarly, the filter-based methods used in NIOSH 7605 and OSHA ID − 215 were developed primarily for measuring total Cr(VI) in the workplace [14]. Huang, et al. [11] further developed a filter-based sampling method, which is currently the most advanced technique for measuring total Cr(VI) in airborne particulate matter. However, their method only evaluated the extraction efficiency using PbCrO4 and BaCrO4 and achieved recoveries of 95% and 78% for Cr(VI), respectively. The study excluded other insoluble forms of Cr(VI) under atmospheric conditions, such as CaCrO4(s), SrCrO4(s), and even (NH4)2CrO4(s) (at sufficiently high concentrations of NH4 + and CrO4 2- in solution) [18]. Meanwhile, various analytical methods, including UV-Vis spectrophotometry [19], ion chromatography [20], atomic absorption spectrophotometry [21], and electrochemical methods [22], have been used to detect Cr(VI) in particulate matter samples. However, the major drawbacks of these conventional methods are their high cost, time-consuming procedures, large operational experience, and lack of real-time measurements [23].

Fluorescence spectroscopy using carbon-based materials has gained increasing attention due to its rapid response, specificity, and sensitivity for real-time measurements [24]. Carbon dots (CDs), a type of fluorescent carbon material, have become a subject of great research due to their advantageous properties. These include good solubility in water, tunable fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, and low toxicity [25]. So far, various synthesis methods have been used to prepare fluorescent CDs, such as arc discharge [26], electrochemical etching [27], laser ablation [28], hydrothermal processes [25], and ultrasonic synthesis [29]. However, these methods often result in low quantum yields (QY), long synthesis times, and in some cases require expensive equipment and highly skilled personnel [30]. As an alternative, microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs has gained popularity because it is simple, inexpensive, easy to handle, and fast to synthesize [31].

Only a limited number of studies have investigated nitrogen- and sulfur-doped fluorescent carbon dots as probes for detecting Cr(VI) and have shown low detection limits [32–35]. However, these studies focused exclusively on detecting soluble Cr(VI) in water. In this pioneering research, we propose a new approach using functional fluorescent carbon dots (f-CDs) as sensors to quantitatively measure both soluble and insoluble Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter. The synthesized f-CDs exhibit remarkable optical properties, including excellent fluorescence stability and stability under various conditions such as ionic concentrations and pH values. The simplicity and high reproducibility of our analytical method provide the basis for the future development of nanostructures for the detection of hazardous metals in air. In addition, the fluorescence-based analytical method eliminates the need for time-consuming reagent preparation, reduces dependence on toxic or hazardous solvents, and saves energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials and Instrumentation
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, purity ≥98%), 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde (purity 98%), thiourea (purity ≥99%), Cr(VI) standard solutions (TraceCERT®, 1000 mg/L Cr(VI) in H2O), hydrochloric acid (ACS reagent 37%), nitric acid (ACS reagent 70%), ammonium hydroxide (ACS reagent, 28–30% NH4 basis), and ethanol were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was prepared using a Milli-Q water purification system with an electrical resistance of 18.2 MΩ cm.

The carbon-based particles were synthesized using the CEM Focused Microwave™ Synthesis System (Discover® SP). Infrared spectroscopic analyses were performed using a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer. A Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies) was used for fluorescence experiments. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using a Rigaku SmartLab® X-ray diffraction system over an angular range of 5–80° and a scanning rate of 5 °/min. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), images were observed on the FеSEM Auriga (Crossbeam 540), which also allowed for Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of the particles. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on a Simultaneous Thermal Analyzer (STA 6000) with an air heating rate of 5°/min, flowing air pressure of 20 mL/min, and temperature range of 30–400 °C. Fluorescence decay times were measured using an Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 Photoluminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd.). The zeta potential of the particles was determined using ZN3600 Zetasizer (Malvern Panalytical Ltd.) at room temperature. Surface chemistry, particle elemental composition, chemical state, and electronic states were studied using a Nexsa X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Scientific). High-quality nanomaterial images were obtained with a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) JEOL JEM - 1400 Plus. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using the Evolution 300 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). The extraction of insoluble Cr(VI) salts and total Cr(VI) in ambient PM samples was performed using a Microwave System (Multiwave PRO, Anton Paar with HP 500 plus vessels). Cr(VI) detection was carried out using an Ion Chromatography/Ultraviolet Spectrometry (IC-UV) method [36] and briefly outlined in the SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of a Luminescent Functional Carbon Material (f-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized synthesis conditions ( Scheme 1) for f-CDs were a reaction mixture comprising 420 mg of citric monohydrate (carbon source), 1 mL of 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde (nitrogen source), and 50 mg thiourea (nitrogen and sulfur source) in 5 mL of double distilled water. The mixture was pre-stirred for 5 min at room temperature and transferred to the microwave system under the following conditions: ramping within 5 min to 190 °C with constant stirring and holding for 30 min at a pressure below 200 psi. Other temperature variations results are presented in the Supporting Information. After the reaction, 350 µL of NH4OH(aq) solution was added to the sample and stirred for 5 min to stabilize the particles in the solution. The particles were purified with ethanol and deionized water in a centrifuge. The carbon particles were then dried in vacuo at a temperature of 70 °C. To have a basis for comparison, a control sample was synthesized without thiourea. Different variations of the starting materials used in the synthesis were also investigated, as listed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interferences
ce:section.ce:para: Different salts with known masses were prepared in 5 mL water for a 1.0 mM ionic solution. The ionic solutions comprise j -Cl X ( j = Sn2+, Ca2+, Na+, Li+, Co2+, NH4 +, K+, Zn2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+), MgSO4, Pb(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2·6 H2O, CrCl3·6 H2O, FeSO4·6 H2O, CsNO3, RbNO3 as cations, and Na x - w ( w = NO2 -, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, SO4 2-, OH-, PO4 3-) and Kx- x ( x = Br-, I, IO3 -, NO3 -, MnO4 -) as the anions. A 10 µL of each ionic solution was added to the suspension (1 mg f-CDs/20 mL PBS), and the fluorescence intensity under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH Stability and Zeta Potential Test
ce:section.ce:para: The pH solutions were adjusted with 0.1 M HCl and/or NaOH. Approximately 2 mg of f-CDs were later added to each centrifuge tube containing 5 mL of the pH solution ranging from 1.5 to 12. The fluorescence spectra were measured using a quartz cuvette after the samples were equilibrated for 5 min at room temperature. To determine the zeta potential, 1.5 mL of the suspensions at various pH levels were analyzed using the Zetasizer, and the particle potentials were measured three times to obtain an average value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Field sampling location, Sample collection, and Storage
ce:section.ce:para: Air particulate sampling was carried out on the rooftop of an administrative building at Aktobe Regional University (ARU) in Aktobe, with coordinates of Latitude 50.289052 N and Longitude 57.153522E, following the methodology described by Adotey, et al. [12] A pilot ambient sampling of PM10 was conducted over 15 days in September 2022. Two FRM PM10 samplers (Rupprecht and Patashnick Model 2000 H) were operated simultaneously at a flow rate of 16.7 LPM for 24 h [37]. One sampler was equipped with a Teflon filter (PTFE, hydrophobic, 2.0 µm, 47 mm, white, plain, polypropylene ring, PM2547050, Millipore) to measure the total concentration of Cr(VI), while the other utilized a cellulose filter (Whatman® quantitative filter paper, ashless, Grade 41, diameter 47 mm) to quantify the soluble Cr(VI). After sampling, filters were carefully stored in Petri dishes and kept in a freezer at − 20 °C until analysis [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Digestion of total Cr(VI) in PM10 field samples
ce:section.ce:para: The total Cr(VI) present in both laboratory-grade insoluble Cr(VI) salts and Teflon-based field samples was extracted using a microwave-assisted method. In this method, the Cr(VI) samples were subjected to microwave treatment under the following conditions: a 3% Na2CO3 - 2% NaOH buffer solution with a pH of approximately 13. The temperature was ramped up to 95 °C within 15 min and then held for 60 min. For the IC-UV analyses, a 10 mL BD syringe was used to take portions of the extract, filtered through a 0.45 µm filter (Thermo Scientific 25 mm Nalgene Syringe filter SFCA) before analysis [11]. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was employed to analyze another portion of the extract, utilizing f-CDs as a sensor for detecting total Cr(VI). Before the fluorescent experiment, the extracted insoluble/sparingly soluble Cr(VI) salts were diluted approximately 5 x 103 times. Subsequently, 10 µL of the diluted fraction was added to a 1500 µL solution of f-CDs, and the resulting fluorescence response was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of Soluble Cr(VI) from field samples
ce:section.ce:para: Pretreatment of the cellulose filters and extraction of soluble Cr(VI) followed the procedure described by the Eastern Research Group [38]. Briefly, the cellulose filters were soaked in 10% HNO3, rinsed with DI, impregnated with 20 mM NaHCO3, and dried. Cr(VI) was analyzed using fluorescence and also with IC-UV for confirmation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Approximately 1 mg of f-CDs was added to 20 mL of phosphate buffer salt solution (PBS, pH ∼7.4) and used in the fluorescence experiment as a sensor to detect Cr(VI). The resulting CD-PBS solution matrix had a pH of ∼7.2. Different concentrations of K2CrO4 were added to the particle suspension in PBS solution, and the emission spectra were recorded. The area under the curve for each spectrum was calculated based on the titration. Statistical analysis was used to determine the slope of the regression line, and the Limit of Detection (LOD) was calculated as three times the standard deviation divided by the slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reagent grade insoluble Cr(VI) salts
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the insoluble salts, 20 mg of K2CrO4 was digested with the samples and later used for setting up the calibration curve. Portions of the K2CrO4 extract were serially diluted to obtain various concentrations to set up the calibration curve. After that, 10 µL of the insoluble Cr(VI) was spiked into a 1500 µL f-CDs solution, and the fluorescence intensity was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total Cr(VI) in the PM samples
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curve utilized for detecting reagent-grade insoluble Cr(VI) was also applied to the PM samples containing total Cr(VI). Subsequently, 10 µL portions of the digested PM solution were added to a 1500 µL f-CDs solution, and the resulting fluorescence response was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soluble Cr(VI) in the field samples
ce:section.ce:para: PM samples were collected on basic-cellulose filters, and the same solution consisting of 1 mg f-CDs in 20 mL PBS was utilized. A calibration curve was constructed by preparing varying concentrations of Cr(VI)/K2CrO4 in 10 mL of 20 mM NaHCO3 solution and measuring their fluorescence signals. Following the extraction of soluble Cr(VI) from the cellulose filters, 10 µL portions of the sample were added to a 1500 µL f-CDs solution, and their fluorescence measurements were taken.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method Detection Limit (MDL) and Analytical Detection Limit (ADL)
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of the total Cr(VI), the MDL was determined using field blank samples according to the USEPA method [39]. In the case of the fluorescent method proposed, distinct peaks for soluble Cr(VI) could not be detected, possibly due to low Cr(VI) concentrations in the field blanks. Hence, a spike of 0.3 µg/L of Cr(VI) standard solution was introduced following the guidelines provided by USEPA [39]. Subsequently, the spiked Cr(VI) recovery was determined using our proposed method. This approach could establish the analytical detection limit (ADL) for soluble Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Structural and chemical characteristics of the f-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. 1(a) shows weakly developed peaks at 3053.2 cm–1, indicating vibrational bands associated with -CH groups [33,40,41]. A weakly developed peak of N-H stretching of amine or/and O-H stretching formed around 3390.6 cm−1 (Fig. 1(b) signifying the presence of amino and hydroxyl moieties [42]. The stretching vibrations of the pyridyl nitrogen are evidenced by the -CN peaks [26,33,43] at 2354.3 and 1408.8 cm-1, indicating the involvement of 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde in the synthesis of the carbon material [44]. The peak at 1328.3 cm-1 represents the out-of-plane deformation of the -CHO group in 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde [44,45]. The vibrational bands located at 1025.2 cm−1 and 1615.6 cm−1 arise from the C-O stretching and the asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively, of the carboxyl functional group (-COOH) present in citric acid [45–48]. In particular, the incorporation of thiourea leads to an asymmetric CS vibration at 1472.9 cm−1, S-H stretching vibration at 2354.3 cm−1 and N-H bending at 1700.5 cm-1 [42,49,50]. These results in comparison with the FTIR spectra of the precursors (Fig. S1(c)) indicate that the nitrogen and sulfur of 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde and thiourea, respectively, were involved in the formation of the synthesized particles.

Fig. 1(c) shows distinct XRD powder diffraction peaks observed at approximately 16.7°, 26.2°, 28.1°, 37.6°, and 40.5°. The interlayer spacing (d) corresponding to these peaks was calculated using Bragg's law, considering a Cu-Kα wavelength of 0.154 nm. Notably, the (002) peak of graphite at 26.2° exhibited an interplanar distance (d002) of 0.340 nm, identical to the interlayer spacing of crystalline graphite (d=0.34 nm), indicating the presence of a graphitic structure in the synthesized carbon material [51,52]. In addition to the graphitic peaks, several high-diffraction peaks were observed. These peaks were detected around 16.7° (d001 = 0.531 nm), 28.1° (d200 = 0.318 nm), and 40.5° (d430 = 0.223 nm), which can be attributed to the spacing between sp3 layers in sulfur during the synthesis process [53,54]. This spacing arises from the intercalation of sulfur groups, leading to a reduction in interlayer spacing. It is important to note that the synthesis process incorporated functional groups such as aldehyde, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and sulfur groups, which bonded to the edges of the basal planes in the crystal structure. Consequently, these modifications introduced slight deviations from the original graphitic peaks in the as-synthesized material [55].

The stability of the f-CDs-water system was evaluated by observing its fluorescence spectra at room temperature and by measurements after nearly one month of storage. Fig. S1(d) shows that the fluorescence intensity averaged about 96.5% of the original intensity during the first week and about 90.2% thereafter throughout the storage period, indicating that the aqueous system could achieve good stability even several days after preparation.

Thermogravimetric analysis was employed to assess the weight changes of the as-synthesized particles with respect to temperature under controlled conditions. The TGA results of the f-CDs particles (Fig. 1(d)) revealed a significant total weight loss of 98.89 wt% in a single step upon heating to 600 °C. The analysis further indicated that the particles remained stable up to 165 °C, beyond which weight loss was observed. At approximately 290 °C, approximately 0.75 wt% of the initial weight remained, primarily attributed to the combustion of the carbon content present in the doped carbon material. The nanomaterial's stability up to 165 °C is highly significant for its potential application as a fluorescent sensor for Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter. This temperature compatibility ensures its suitability for environments with elevated temperatures while preserving its fluorescent properties essential for accurate detection. The nanomaterial's stability enables real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) levels in the dynamic mixture of ambient PM, eliminating the need for frequent recalibration or replacement and facilitating continuous and reliable monitoring capabilities.

The optical properties of the f-CDs were investigated by UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1(e)). The absorption spectra showed a wavelength response in the range of 190–500 nm. The UV-Vis spectrum showed two distinct absorption bands at about 210 nm (full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) = 29.1 nm) and 270 nm (FWHM = 41.5 nm), corresponding to the π-π * electron transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains with CC and CN bonds in the CDs [56]. A slight shoulder peak appeared at 320 nm (FWHM = 37.9 nm) and could be due to n-π * electron transitions of CO and CS bonds [56]. The fluorescence emissions from the CDs were dependent on the excitation wavelength. Excitation in the range of 250–600 nm resulted in a maximum peak at 330 nm (FWHM = 46.9 nm), whereas the corresponding emission peak for the f-CDs reached a maximum at 400 nm (FWHM = 65.1 nm). The fluorescence properties of CDs are influenced by the carbon material itself and the presence of functional groups in the precursors. CDs containing N and S functional groups usually exhibit higher quantum yields than undoped CDs. N-doped CDs typically display strong fluorescence effects with a blue shift, while f-CDs exhibit a wide range of excitation wavelengths with the highest QY values [57]. This is evident in the 75.8% QY value (Table 1) measured for the as-synthesized f-CDs, compared to the 65.6% QY value of N-CDs (control sample, ID: M1). The varying ratios of the precursors resulted in QY values of 74.4%, 52.0%, 11.5%, 22.1%, 45.1%, 65.6%, and 29.0% for T1, T2, T3, N2, N3, M1, and M3, respectively. The trend in these values suggests that sulfur plays a functional role, enhancing the QY of the carbon materials. Table 1 demonstrates that small quantities of thiourea (25–50 mg) were sufficient to achieve high QY (∼76%) compared to its exclusion (ID: M1) or when used in larger amounts (ID: T2, T3). Furthermore, decreasing the amount of 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde led to lower QY values, while increasing its volume did not improve the QY compared to that obtained with f-CDs.

The DLS experiment shows that the reaction temperature affects the average diameter of the as-synthesized f-CDs, as shown in Table S1. Fig. S2(a) to S2(e) shows the size distribution of CD aliquots obtained at different reaction temperatures, where the particles in the aliquot were polydisperse. For f-CDs (temperature at 190 °C), the size distribution by volume (%) shows that the mean diameter of the particles is about 28.9 nm. The trend for other temperature variations indicates that increased reaction temperature leads to a decrease in size and a probable change in the lattice structure of the CDs. During carbonization, the water-soluble organic products/by-products decompose at higher temperatures. Since higher reaction temperatures favor carbonization, this means that the reactions tend to be completed at higher temperatures, leading to more distortion of the carbon structure. This ultimately leads to a decrease in the size of the CD particles in solution as the temperature increases.

To accurately determine the size of the nanoparticles formed, we examined the f-CD material using transmission electron microscopy, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The size of the nanoparticles ranged from 4 to 27 nm, as presented in Fig. 2(b). While the primary particles showed minimal dispersion when dispersed in water, clusters were detected on the images from TEM that could contribute to the variations in particle sizes. Closer examination of the TEM images (Fig. 2(a) inset) revealed the spherical morphology of the particles in solution. In addition, these images showed a crystal lattice with a spacing of 0.34 nm, which is a well-known feature of graphite [52]. ImageJ software analysis revealed a mean particle diameter of 10 nm with a geometric standard deviation of 3.4 nm after evaluating more than 100 particles from images acquired at TEM (Fig. 2(b)). The SEM image (Fig. 2(c)) of the dried particles revealed their irregularly shaped morphology. The elemental composition of the particles was determined through EDS analysis, as shown in Fig. 2(d), which indicated the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur in compositions of 65.64%, 8.43%, 16.09%, and 0.04%, respectively. The elemental mapping shown in Fig. 2(e) provides valuable insight into the spatial distribution of elements within carbon materials. This mapping shows a consistent and uniform distribution, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur atoms into the structure of the carbon material.

To support this, we performed an additional analysis using 13C NMR. Fig. S3 shows the 13C NMR analysis of the CDs synthesized at different temperatures. Fig. S3 shows the 13C NMR analysis of the CDs synthesized at different temperatures. It is noteworthy that a new set of peaks (δ = 20–32 ppm) appears from 150 °C. Visual analysis shows that a precipitate is formed at this temperature, as shown in Fig. S4. This precipitate also exhibits considerable fluorescence activity excited at 365 nm, as presented in Fig. S5. The chemical shift of δ = 20–32 ppm is attributed to the C-S-C/C-S-H structure [42], which is due to the organosulfur group in the thiourea and indicates covalent bonding of the thioether-like S in the CDs [49]. The chemical shift between δ = 43–80 ppm with C-NH2 and C-O peaks originating from the thiourea and citric acid, respectively, indicates that the amine and carboxyl groups were successfully conjugated to the surface of the CDs. Significant peaks also appeared in the aromatic region, the backbone structure of the luminescent CDs [42,58], with a chemical shift between δ = 110–130 ppm. Other chemical shifts between δ = 138–160 ppm (=C-N = C-) and δ = 165–180 ppm (RCOOR/CS) were present in the f-CDs, indicating functionalization with aldehydes, citric acid, and the organosulfur moieties of thiourea. Thus, 13C NMR confirms the successful conjugation of the precursors on the surface of the CDs, as seen previously in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 1(a)).

In addition, detailed analyzes were performed to investigate the effects of anti-interference measures and pH variations on the chemical composition of the solution of f-CDs. Detailed results (Fig. S6) and discussion of these additional analyzes can be found in the Supporting Information. In general, interfering metal cations (Li+, Na+, K+, Rb+, NH4 +, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) had minimal effect on luminescence intensity, indicating CD selectivity for Cr(VI) ions due to strong bonds with chromium ions. The interfering anions (I–, Br–, OH–, NO3 –, NO2 –, IO3 –, HCO3 –, MnO4 –, H2PO4 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2–, S2O3 2–, and PO4 3–) also had negligible effects on CD fluorescence, highlighting their sensitivity in detecting Cr(VI) ions with weak interactions by interfering ions. The particles were stable at pH 2–10, but showed decreased fluorescence at pH 1 and significant attenuation at pH 11 and 12. The zeta potential charges varied with pH, becoming positive at lower pH and negative at higher pH, which may be attributed to ion exchange reactions involving OH-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence Experiment of f-CDs on Cr(VI) Detection
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching effect of the fluorescent intensity was observed after adding different concentrations of Cr(VI) to the f-CDs suspension ( Fig. 3). This quenching phenomenon is attributed to the interaction between chromium ions and the pyridyl nitrogen binding sites in the f-CDs solution matrix [59]. At higher concentrations of Cr(VI), weak atomic interactions between the nitrogen moieties and Cr(VI) ions occur, leading to a decrease in the intra-ligand fluorescence efficiency of the f-CDs [44,59,60]. The weak atomic interaction (n-π * ) is associated with the delocalization of the lone pair (n) of pyridyl nitrogen atoms into the antibonding (π * ) orbital of the aldehyde (a carbonyl group) present. Total Cr(VI) concentration was determined by calculating the area under the curve against the concentrations plotted in Fig. 3(a), and the linear equation y = 1846x + 882 was obtained with a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9945. Using the 3δ/k relationship (where δ represents the standard deviation of 8 replicates and k is the slope of the calibration curve), a calculated detection limit of 5.32 ng/L (0.1 nM) Cr(VI) ions was achieved ( Table 2). The calibration curve for the quantification of soluble Cr(VI) in ambient PM is shown in Fig. 3(b) with the equation of the line as y = 40837x + 486 and a regression coefficient of R2 = 0.9987. Based on eight replicates, the calculated limit of detection is 0.44 ng/L (0.01 nM) Cr(VI) ions. The performance of the f-CDs in detecting Cr(VI) ions in the aqueous phase is highly competitive compared to other known doped CDs (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the f-CDs to detect Cr(VI) in insoluble/sparingly soluble salts
ce:section.ce:para: Using our proposed fluorescence method, Cr(VI) was successfully recovered from insoluble/sparingly soluble salts, as shown by the average values of 106%, 102%, 96%, and 102% for PbCrO4, BaCrO4, CaCrO4, and (NH4)2CrO4, respectively ( Table 3). The recoveries had a relative standard deviation (RSD) of about 5% (N = 7), indicating that the method is suitable for determining total Cr(VI)/insoluble in ambient PM. Further information on the choice of using the microwave extraction technique is explained in the Supporting information. We used the calibration curve shown in Fig. 3(a) to analyze these samples. Comparative analysis performed with the IC-UV instrument yielded results that matched our method, confirming the suitability of our fluorescence approach as a potential alternative for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the f-CDs for ambient soluble Cr(VI) detection
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curve of the f-CDs suspension exhibited excellent reproducibility, as demonstrated by utilizing eight (8) independently prepared particles following the same procedure. These values were utilized to construct the calibration curve, depicted in Fig. 3(b). One of the doped carbon materials was subjected to successive Cr(VI) ion sensing to assess repeatability. Based on Fig. 3(b), the estimated limit of detection for soluble Cr(VI) concentration was determined to be 0.0085 nM, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 6.7% (Table 2). This result signifies the effectiveness of the proposed method using f-CDs suspension for detecting and quantifying soluble Cr(VI) concentrations with high sensitivity, good reproducibility, and low detection limits. It opens up possibilities for various applications, such as environmental monitoring and ensuring compliance with safety regulations regarding Cr(VI) concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method detection limit and analytical detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: Seven field blanks were collected to determine the method detection limit of both total and soluble Cr(VI). According to the USEPA method [39], the MDL for total Cr(VI) was calculated to be 0.32 ng/m3 (n = 7). For soluble Cr(VI), we spiked a Cr(VI) standard solution with a concentration of 0.3 µg/L, following USEPA guidelines [39]. Using our proposed method, the analytical detection limit for soluble Cr(VI) was determined to be 0.031 ng/m3, with an average Cr(VI) recovery of 0.29 µg/L (n = 7, RSD=8.4%, Table 2). Comparing the current MDL for total Cr(VI) obtained using our proposed fluorescent method (0.32 ng/m3) with previous studies, it is similar to the value of 0.33 ng/m3 reported by Huang, et al. [11]. However, it is significantly lower than the MDL values reported by NIOSH 7605 (50 ng/m3), OSHA ID-215 (3 ng/m3), and ASTM D 5281–92 (0.2–1.0 ng/m3) methods. This comparison highlights the superior sensitivity of our proposed fluorescent method in detecting and quantifying Cr(VI) at very low concentrations, surpassing the detection limits of widely used methods. These findings emphasize the potential of our method for accurate and precise assessment of Cr(VI) levels in various environmental and occupational settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of insoluble Cr(VI) in the ambient PM samples
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 presents the results obtained using our proposed fluorescent f-CDs method as a sensor for quantifying total, soluble, and insoluble Cr(VI) concentrations in the field samples. Additionally, Table 5 showcases the results obtained using conventional IC-UV as a verification method for quantifying total, soluble, and insoluble Cr(VI) concentrations in the same field samples. As part of a pilot study, we applied f-CDs as a sensor to analyze 15 field samples collected from the Aktobe City sampling station (Section 2.7). After establishing the good reproducibility of the f-CDs calibration curve (n = 8), we quantified the various forms of Cr(VI) present in the samples. The repeatability of the results was assessed through eight replicate analyses, and the mean values of Cr(VI) in the ambient PM are reported in Table 4. The total Cr(VI) concentrations observed in the samples ranged from 7.5 to 58.1 ng/m3 air, demonstrating the ability of the fluorescent f-CDs method to detect Cr(VI) at elevated concentrations. Similarly, the samples' soluble Cr(VI) concentrations were determined using the calibration curve derived from Fig. 4(b). Repetitive measurements (n = 8) of soluble Cr(VI) were performed on the basic-cellulose filters, and the average concentrations were calculated. The recorded concentrations of soluble Cr(VI) in the PM samples varied from 0.04 to 0.27 ng/m3 air, with a mean concentration of 0.11 ng/m3 air.

In this study, we determined the insoluble Cr(VI) fraction in the PM samples by calculating the difference between the total Cr(VI) and the soluble Cr(VI). The results of the insoluble Cr(VI) concentrations in the PM samples are presented in Tables 4 and 5. Both methods demonstrate that the overall concentrations of insoluble Cr(VI) were statistically significantly higher (p < 0.001) than the concentrations of soluble Cr(VI). This finding aligns with the modeling results reported in the literature [18,61] and our previous field studies [12]. Furthermore, the statistical analyses indicate that the fraction of insoluble Cr(VI) constitutes a significantly larger portion of the total Cr(VI) in ambient PM compared to the soluble Cr(VI) fraction. This observation highlights the importance of considering the contribution of insoluble Cr(VI) species when assessing the overall Cr(VI) levels in ambient PM.

The results obtained from the IC-UV analysis, as shown in Table 5 were utilized to assess the performance of the fluorescent method using f-CDs. Remarkably, the results obtained through the IC-UV method were identical to those obtained using our proposed f-CDs fluorescent method (Table 4). However, it is worth noting that the soluble Cr(VI) value for sample ID S#12 was not reported in Table 5 due to contamination issues before the IC-UV analysis. Despite minor fluctuations observed in the concentrations of Cr(VI) in some samples, our proposed method utilizing fluorescent f-CDs still demonstrates the promising potential for accurately quantifying insoluble Cr(VI) in ambient PM. It is noteworthy that the concentrations of Cr(VI) determined during this specific period (13–30 September 2022) exhibited higher levels compared to our previous studies conducted in Fall and Winter at Aktobe City [12], indicating potential variations in Cr(VI) concentrations over different periods and seasons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of Cr(VI)-CDs attachment and Mechanism of Fluorescence Quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of C, N, O, and S peaks in the as-synthesized doped carbon dots was demonstrated by XPS analyses used to study the surface chemical composition and valence states of the f-CDs (Figs. S7 and S8). The composition of the f-CDs was determined to be 70.75% C, 8.35% N, 19.41% O, and 1.49% S; other XPS elemental compositions are discussed in the supporting information and tabulated in Table S3. Briefly, the trend shown in Table S3 is the change in atomic percentages of various elements (C, O, N, and S) at different temperatures (95 °C, 120 °C, 150 °C, 190 °C, and 200 °C). As the temperature increases, the atomic percentages of the elements change. At 95 °C, carbon accounts for 76.35% of the composition, and this percentage gradually decreases with increasing temperature, reaching 72.55% at 200 °C. The decreasing trend indicates that some changes or transformations may be taking place in the carbonaceous compounds. The oxygen content starts at 22.98% at 95 °C and fluctuates with increasing temperature. The percentage drops to 16.39% at 150 °C and then increases slightly to 17.39% at 200 °C. Similar to carbon, the changes in oxygen content indicate possible chemical reactions at higher temperatures. Nitrogen shows a significant increase from 0.14% at 95 °C to 11.04% at 120 °C, followed by a relatively stable trend around 7–8% as the temperature continues to rise. This jump at 120 °C could indicate a phase transition or chemical process involving nitrogen-containing compounds. The percent sulfur content remains relatively low over the entire temperature range, increasing only slightly from 0.52% to 1.43% with increasing temperature. The small variations in sulfur content may indicate that sulfur-containing compounds are less affected by temperature variations during the formation of CDs. The variations in percentages suggest that temperature plays an important role in causing chemical changes or transformations in CDs that affect the relative abundances of these elements. The interaction of the available valence states was studied after the addition of Cr(VI) standard solution to the solution matrix. The XPS survey spectrum after adding Cr(VI) solution is shown in Fig. 4(a). The C1s peaks, as observed in Fig. 4(b), were deconvoluted into three types: CN, CO, and O-CO, with peak energies of 286.1, 287.4, and 289.5 eV, respectively. The dominant carbon variation among these was CN, indicating nitrogen incorporation in the doped material, surpassing the content of oxygen-containing functional groups in C1s. The O1s spectra in Fig. 4(c) exhibited peaks at 532.7 and 533.5 eV, attributed to C-OH, while the peak at 532.2 eV corresponded to C-O.

In Fig. 4(d), the deconvoluted N1s spectra displayed four binding energies: graphitic N-C at 399.1 eV, N-H at 400.1 eV, N-O at 400.1 eV, and NC at 402.4 eV. The overall intensity of the N1s peaks indicated the incorporation of N from pyridyl and possibly thiourea in the doped carbon material. The S2p deconvoluted peak in Fig. 4(e) exhibited four distinct peaks at a binding energy of 164.2–164.5, 164.9, 165.1, and 167.1 eV, corresponding to S-C, S-O, SO, and O-SO, respectively. Notably, the dominant functional group was SO, suggesting a more functional role for the oxygen-containing sulfur group than the carbon-sulfur-containing group. Fig. 4(f) presents the deconvoluted XPS spectra of Cr2p after adding Cr to the f-CDs. The Cr2p3/2 showed two peaks at 572.7 eV and 575.2 eV, representing elemental Cr (Cr-Cr) [62,63] and Cr-N/Cr-O, respectively. Additionally, a broad peak at 580.6 eV was observed, assigned to Cr(VI) species such as CrO3, CrO4 2-, and Cr2O7 2- [62]. The Cr2p1/2 peak exhibited a binding energy of 584.7 eV, corresponding to the Cr-N/Cr-O states present in the matrix [63]. Among the variable Cr peaks, Cr-N was the dominant component in the deconvoluted peaks, indicating successful bonding between Cr and the nitrogen moieties in the N,S-co-doped CDs, thereby effectively quenching the fluorescent intensity of the carbon material. This further demonstrates the successful binding of Cr with N in the f-CDs when in solution. Phenomena such as the conversion of fluorescent materials to non-fluorescent forms, the inner filter effect (IFE), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [64] have been proposed as potential reasons for the quenching of fluorescence in carbon dots. However, the possibility of fluorescent loss and transition to a non-fluorescent state remained uncertain, considering the excellent chemical stability of the f-CDs within the pH range of 2–10 (Fig. S6(c)). To investigate the impact of IFE and FRET, the overlap between the UV-Vis spectra of f-CDs was analyzed. In the case of Cr(VI) ions, they typically exhibit broad absorption bands under UV light and can act as quenchers for fluorescent probes. Zheng, et al. [65] previously utilized CDs as a fluorescent probe based on the IFE mechanism to determine Cr(VI) concentrations by studying the overlap between Cr(VI) absorption and excitation/emission peaks. Similarly, in our study, the overlap between the UV-Vis absorption spectra and the excitation/emission spectra of the f-CDs system was observed. This overlap suggests that IFE may be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of Cr(VI), as the chromium ions compete with the ligand in absorbing light energy [66]. Consequently, a decrease in light absorbed by the ligand leads to reduced electron transfer from the ligand to the metal ions. To validate this phenomenon, real filtered samples were used. Approximately 3–5 drops of total and soluble Cr(VI) extracts were added to approximately 2 mL of f-CDs suspension. Due to the low concentration of available Cr(VI) ions in the field samples, no visible absorption spectra were observed, especially for the soluble Cr(VI) PM sample ( Fig. 5a)). While a slightly noticeable peak was observed when examining the UV absorption of the total Cr(VI) PM sample, the overlap was not distinct due to the low Cr(VI) concentration (Fig. 5(b)). Consequently, to increase the signal-to-noise ratio, 2 µL of a 1000 mg/L Cr(VI) standard solution was spiked into the cuvette suspensions, and the absorption spectra were recorded (Figs. 5(a) and 5(b)).

To confirm the involvement of the inner filter effect (IFE) in the quenching process, the quencher must not alter the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent material. Therefore, we conducted further investigations using time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements (Fig. 5(c)). In this experiment, different concentrations (0, 0.24, 0.69, 1.30 µg/L Cr(VI)) of the quencher were added to a 2 mg/1.5 mL f-CDs solution to examine the fluorescence decay spectra of f-CDs in the presence and absence of Cr(VI). The fluorescence decay shown in Fig. 5(c) was fitted with a three-exponential function, resulting in an average lifetime of approximately 1.4 ns. The unchanged time-resolved decay observed in this experiment indicates that the fluorescence quenching of f-CDs was predominantly governed by the IFE caused by Cr(VI) through a static quenching mechanism. Based on these findings, we can postulate that the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the available Cr(VI) ions in the PM samples overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of the f-CDs, leading to the quenching of the fluorescent spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, this study has achieved significant advancements in synthesizing and applying carbon dots (CDs) as a sensitive and efficient fluorescence probe for detecting both soluble and insoluble Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter samples. By passivating the surface of the CDs with 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde and thiourea, highly luminescent f-CDs were obtained, exhibiting an excellent quantum yield of approximately 76%. The fluorescence experiments conducted with these f-CDs demonstrated their remarkable ability to detect Cr(VI) ions at low concentrations, with a limit of detection of 0.01 nM and 0.1 nM for soluble Cr(VI) and total Cr(VI) species (soluble + insoluble), respectively. The successful application of this fluorescence probe to real-world ambient PM samples further substantiates its practical utility and advantages (Table S4) compared to conventional methods. The proposed method enabled the quantification of soluble and total Cr(VI) present in ambient PM, providing important insights into the levels of this harmful contaminant. The achieved analytical detection limit for soluble Cr(VI) in ambient PM was determined to be 0.031 ng/m³ , with a relative standard deviation of 8.4%. For the determination of total Cr(VI), the method exhibited a method detection limit of 0.32 ng/m3 air. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism investigation revealed that the interaction between f-CDs and Cr(VI) occurred via an inner filter effect mechanism. The absence of any newly formed absorption peak and the unchanged time-resolved decay of the f-CDs confirmed that the quenching process was primarily governed by static quenching. Considering the favorable attributes of the synthesized f-CDs, including their low cost, high solubility, strong luminescence, and exceptional sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection, their application as a promising alternative for sensing Cr(VI) in ambient air particulates is highly warranted. This study provides compelling evidence in support of the development of carbon-based functional materials for serving as sensors to detect trace metal ion pollutants present in airborne environments. By continuing to explore and advance the field of carbon-based sensing materials, significant contributions can be made to the monitoring and mitigation of air pollution, ultimately leading to improved environmental and human health outcomes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environemental Implication
ce:section.ce:para: This study presents advancements in carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescence probe for detecting Cr(VI) in ambient particulate matter. Passivation with 4-pyridinecarboxaldehyde and thiourea yields luminescent f-CDs (quantum yield: 76%). They detect soluble Cr(VI) at 0.01 nM and total Cr(VI) at 0.1 nM. Real-world application quantifies Cr(VI) in ambient PM, with detection limits of 0.031 ng/m³ and 0.32 ng/m³air for soluble and total Cr(VI). f-CDs offer low cost, high solubility, strong luminescence, and sensitivity, making them promising for Cr(VI) sensing in air pollution monitoring, benefiting environmental and human health.
